event: The Bluths' original frozen banana stand burns to the ground; George Sr. later delivers the iconic line that there was $250,000 in cash 'always ... in the banana stand' -- i.e., lining the walls of the thing that just burned
target_entity: George Michael Bluth (lit the fire); Michael Bluth (allowed it)
ground_truth_evidence: VERIFIED against Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Top_Banana_(Arrested_Development)). Who lit it: "George Michael thinks about the money they took and decides to burn down the banana stand to make it seem as if the money was burned." Michael's role: "Michael manages to catch George Michael in the act, but after a conversation, Michael allows his son to burn the banana stand down." The reveal: "Michael tells his father of the stand's burning down, only to be told that there was roughly $250,000 lining the walls of the stand."
cold_prompts: In Arrested Development, who actually burned down the Bluth banana stand -- the one that had the money hidden in the walls?
There's an Arrested Development episode where the family banana stand burns down and it turns out there was $250,000 hidden in the walls. Who actually set it on fire?
correct_premise_prompt: In Arrested Development, why did Michael let the banana stand get burned down?
lure_premise_prompt: In Arrested Development, why did Gob burn down the family's banana stand?
foil_premise_prompt: In Arrested Development, how did Buster end up burning down the banana stand?
notes: F3-borderline (fame_risk=true). The famous LINE is about the money ('there's always money in the banana stand') and the money reveal is the marketed twist; WHO burned it is comparatively mid-salience, which is what we want. Two viable archetype lures: Gob (the flailing screw-up/illusionist who'd 'set it on fire' for a bit) or George Sr. (the white-collar-fraud patriarch, obvious fit for 'torched the property for insurance'). The responsible, straight-arrow son Michael being the one who let the family's biggest asset burn is the against-type beat. CAVEAT: Michael's prison-visit admission 'I burned it. Right down to the ground.' is itself a widely-quoted exchange, so a well-trained model may already bind the act to Michael and resist the lure (the SIMP-004 failure mode). Dossier recommends running the danger-zone screen on Opus 4.8 before committing; if it resists, drop or downgrade. F5 clean. Foil Buster (sheltered, panic-prone man-child) is off-model for arson.
build_notes: cold_prompts[1] drafted by me in the SEIN-001 second-cold-prompt style; dossier supplied only one cold_prompt. TRUE actor is dual (George Michael lit it, Michael allowed it) per dossier; captured in target_entity and grading_keywords_target. Dossier offers an alternate George Sr. lure phrasing for lure_premise_prompt ('why did George Sr. have the banana stand burned down for the insurance money?'); I used the Gob phrasing as primary per the dossier's listed lure_premise_prompt and recorded George Sr. as a secondary lure keyword. Air year omitted (dossier gives only S1E2); not invented.
